dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 7 responding for stroke call at VCU Medical Building.
Hello sir, my name is Jordan and I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Oh, the patient was showing signs of a stroke-he couldn't speak clearly and had a droopy face. Staff brought him out quickly.
Can you tell me your name sir?
No response, he keeps coughing but is awake and alert.
His eyes are open, but he has a very odd gaze to the right side.
You know your name sir? Can you say it for us?
(Gurgling cough only)
Can you hold my finger with your left hand sir?
No, he tries but immediately drops the hand.
Does he have any difficulty speaking or coughing?
No, he coughs during attempts, so I think his airway is clear.
His breathing looks normal. Is it anything unusual now?
No, not that we've seen.
His breathing is fast at 25, let me check his oxygen with the pulse oximeter now.
His oxygen level is 81%, which is low. We should give him some air.
Let's give him 2 liters of oxygen via nasal cannula. He'll need more oxygen support.
His respirations slightly increased to 26 breaths a minute. We'll keep monitoring.
Pulse is strong and regular. I'll take a blood pressure now.
His blood pressure is 165 over 99. We've recorded that.
Skin is warm, dry, with no specified cyanosis in notes, though we cannot rule it out without observation.
Is there any recent illness or surgery you should be aware of?
Yes, he had a moment earlier today where he just stared off, but then it passed on its own. He has a known history of multiple strokes and dementia.
When did the symptoms begin today?
About 20 minutes ago. He was sitting in his wheelchair when he suddenly appeared altered.
Sir, do you have any pain right now or has he been experiencing any?
He moaned when we moved his left arm, but is otherwise not in pain.
The skin is dry and non-diaphoretic. We don't see rashes or cuts noted, though further examination should be done.
Facial droop is present, specifically left-sided. He's leaning to the left and cannot smile. Pupils are reactive and equal at 2 mm each.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; EMT
dialogue.utterance: We found no signs of obstruction, no discharge, and he doesn't appear to struggle with swallowing.
Lung fields are clear on both sides, no wheezes or crackles identified. Heart rhythm appears regular on palpation.
We did not note any complaints of abdominal pain or signs of distress at this time.
Pulse is now at 78 and remains regular with normal strength.
Blood glucose is 148-elevated but not life-threatening at this moment.
Jordan, we need to follow stroke protocol as this includes facial droop, altered speech, and motor weakness on one side.
I'll begin an IV line in the left forearm now. Let's prepare supplies.
We'll start 4-channel ECG monitoring to confirm sinus rhythm and evaluate cardiac involvement.
First IV in right AC was unsuccessful. I'll switch to left AC for a successful attempt.
Pulse is currently at 90, SpO2 is 98, BP is 159/102. All are consistent with ongoing vitals.
Transport to VCU ED with a stroke alert. Are there any preferences for the facility?
No, we don't have any preference for where he goes.
We will carefully lift him from the wheelchair and transfer to a stretcher with 5-point harness for stability.
Patient is now securely fastened with a five-point harness and is ready for transport.
He shows signs consistent with a stroke-facial droop, poor left-sided function, and altered mental status. GCS is 12.
He has a background of multiple strokes, seizures, and dementia with behavioral disturbance.
He was not in distress during transport, though moaned slightly when his left arm was touched.
Double-checking vitals before transport safely proceeds. No dramatic changes noted at this moment.
Respirations remain at 26. All parameters are stable and monitoring is ongoing.
Blood pressure shows no major concerns-159/102 with no significant drop detected so far.
Glucose was not recordable due to limitations noted. IV remains active and oxygen is being delivered.
A second IV placed in the left AC with 10 ml saline flush. He is ready for emergencies if needed.
No other medications are being administered at the moment.
He was reportedly normal earlier, aside from a brief staring period that resolved.
Mental status is clearly altered now, with facial droop and left-sided weakness. Our suspicion for stroke remains high.
He is on his way to VCU ED with a stroke alert status so they're aware of the incoming patient.
Hospital transport team and CT received the alert. They're prepared upon arrival.
He is 81, with suprapubic renal stage 3 disease and history of abdominal aortic aneurysm. A full report is sent for context.
He presented with dysconjugate gaze, altered motor control, and abnormal verbal response-clear neurological deficits.
Further following stroke protocol steps until we reach the hospital. No new facts contradict the initial suspicion.
Final checks-harness intact, IV running, vitals stabilized, all ready for handover.
Currently no new findings. All care steps up to this point were conducted with protocol compliance.
We are aware of all meds and allergies based on staff. No changes noted from previous checks.
Bags are full and vitals are strong. We're ready for the final handoff to ED staff upon arrival.
Once we arrive, the staff in CT will be expecting a patient with possible stroke and neurological changes per our report.
Current vitals, monitored care, and all interventions remain consistent with current transport protocol.
Time to arrival is estimated, and ED is alerted for a rapid stroke patient admission and assessment.
EKG monitoring and IV are in progress. No further interventions needed at this time.
Pain is minimal and localized to the left arm when manipulated. No increase in distress observed.
Target facility is VCU for stroke alert patient. Choice is confirmed via mobile data system and hospital tracking.
We'll remain on stroke protocol until final handoff at the hospital. No deviations are needed.
